author: Dr. Jennifer Adams
content:
TELEPHONE ENCOUNTER — 11/16/2025, 4:22 PM

Patient: Robert Morrison (MRN: MRN-78432156)
DOB: 3/15/1952 (Age 73)
Callback number verified: (555) 482-7136

REASON FOR CALL:
Patient called to discuss recent lab results and provide a symptom update. His wife Patricia initiated the call and handed the phone to Mr. Morrison.

CLINICAL DISCUSSION:
Reviewed November 2025 labs drawn 11/12/2025:
- BMP: Cr 1.98, eGFR 35.4, K 4.84, Na 135, HCO3 23, BUN 42, Glucose 148
- A1c: 7.4% (improved from 7.8% in October 2024 — empagliflozin benefit continuing)
- CBC: Hgb 11.0, Hct 33.2, WBC 5.8, Plt 192
- BNP: 400 (improved from 510 in October)
- Lipid panel: Total cholesterol 168, LDL 82, HDL 38, Triglycerides 198

I reviewed each result with the patient. His kidney function remains stable at CKD Stage 3b. His potassium at 4.84 is elevated but below our 5.0 threshold for intervention — I counseled him to continue limiting high-potassium foods (bananas, oranges, potatoes, tomatoes) and to avoid salt substitutes containing potassium chloride. His A1c at 7.4% continues to be at our target, and I praised his compliance with his diabetes regimen. His BNP has improved from 510 to 400, suggesting his heart failure is better controlled since reaching full quadruple GDMT with empagliflozin. His hemoglobin at 11.0 is stable — within the range expected for CKD-related anemia.

His wife then expressed concern about his mood. She reports he has been less interested in activities he previously enjoyed — he has stopped attending church choir practice (weekly for the past 15 years), has declined several invitations from his grandson to attend basketball games, and spends most of his time watching television. He reports sleeping poorly, waking early (4-5 AM) and unable to fall back asleep even when not getting up to urinate. His appetite has decreased, and Patricia notes he is eating smaller portions. When asked directly, Mr. Morrison acknowledges feeling 'down' but attributes it to 'getting old and being tired of doctors.' He denies any thoughts of self-harm or suicide. He denies any feelings of hopelessness or worthlessness but does admit to feeling like a 'burden' on his wife given her role managing his medications and appointments.

I administered a PHQ-2 by telephone:
- Little interest or pleasure in doing things: 1 (several days)
- Feeling down, depressed, or hopeless: 1 (several days)
- Total: 2/6 — subthreshold but concerning in clinical context

I recommended an in-office visit for formal PHQ-9 screening and further evaluation. I discussed that depression is common in patients with chronic illnesses, particularly heart failure, and that effective treatments are available. Patient is ambivalent but agrees to come in. Wife is supportive and will ensure he attends.

Patient also reports his daily weights have been stable (97-97.5 kg range), his leg swelling is at baseline (trace by evening), and his neuropathy symptoms are unchanged at 4/10. He has had no falls since the September incident. Annual flu shot was given at his last visit.

PLAN:
- No medication changes at this time
- Schedule in-office visit within 4-6 weeks for depression screening (PHQ-9), comprehensive physical exam, and annual wellness component
- Continue all current medications
- Continue daily weights, low-sodium diet, fluid restriction
- Monitor potassium — if next value >5.0, will consider reducing spironolactone
- Patient instructed to call or present to ED for worsening SOB, chest pain, weight gain >3 lbs/day, or any stroke symptoms
- Wife counseled on stroke warning signs (BE-FAST: Balance, Eyes, Face, Arms, Speech, Time)

Call duration: 24 minutes. Spoke primarily with patient, wife present for portion of call regarding mood concerns.
attestation:
Telephone encounter documented.

Electronically signed by: Dr. Jennifer Adams
Date: 11/16/2025
